title: Last Price, Change and Percent Change
html:
The three numbers everyone sees first

Pull up any stock quote and the top line shouts three things at you: the last price, the change, and the percent change. They look trivial, but most beginners misread them, anchoring on a green or red number without knowing what it is measured against. Let us define each precisely and compute them by hand.

Last price

The last price is simply the price at which the most recent trade actually executed. It is not the price you are guaranteed to get. If you place a market order to buy, you pay the ask; if you sell, you receive the bid. The last price sits somewhere between them and is already in the past the instant it prints. During the trading day it updates tick by tick; after the close it freezes at the closing print until the next session.

Definition.
Change = Last price − Prior close. Percent change = (Last price − Prior close) ÷ Prior close × 100.

The reference point is the PRIOR CLOSE, not the open

This is the single most common error. The green or red number is measured from yesterday's official closing price, not from today's opening trade. A stock can open sharply lower on bad overnight news, grind higher all day, and still show red if it never recovers to the prior close. The intraday action and the headline change number can tell opposite stories.

Worked example

ACME last printed at 142.50. Yesterday's official close was 139.30. Compute the change and percent change step by step.

ACME · change worksheet
Last price 142.50 Prior close 139.30 Change = 142.50 − 139.30 = +3.20 Percent = 3.20 ÷ 139.30 = 0.02297 × 100 = +2.30%

So ACME is up 3.20 points, or +2.30%. Notice the percent is what lets you compare moves across stocks of different prices. A +3.20 move means very different things on a 14-dollar stock (huge) than on a 1,400-dollar stock (a rounding error). Always think in percent when comparing.

Why the same dollar move is not the same move

Same +3.20, three stocks
NOVA @ 16.00 prior → +3.20 = +20.0% ACME @ 139.30 prior → +3.20 = +2.30% ORCA @ 1280 prior → +3.20 = +0.25%

The dollar change is identical; the meaning is wildly different. The percent normalizes for price, which is exactly why it earns its spot on the quote.

Reading the chart

The candlestick below shows ACME's recent session in context. The most recent up-bar carries the price to 142.50, comfortably above the prior close marked as support.

The horizontal line at 139.30 is the reference the entire headline number is computed from. Every candle that closes above it would show green for the day; every candle below would show red. The body of the final candle measures the day's own range, while the headline change measures only the gap from that one horizontal line to the last tick.

Insight.
Pre-market and after-hours quotes often show a SECOND change number measured against the regular-session close. Do not confuse an after-hours +1.5% with the regular-session +2.30%; they have different reference points and different (thin) liquidity.
Pitfall.
A big green percent on a stock you do not follow can be a low-priced ticker reacting to a single news item on tiny volume. Percent change alone never tells you whether a move is trustworthy. That is why the very next number we read is volume.
▸Try It

Goal: compute change and percent change from raw quote data.

  1. Pick any stock and write down its last price and its prior close (labeled "previous close" on most quotes).
  2. Subtract to get the dollar change. Confirm the sign matches the green/red on the page.
  3. Divide the change by the prior close and multiply by 100. Match it to the displayed percent.
  4. Now find a stock priced under 10 dollars and one over 500. For each, work out what a +2.00 dollar move would be in percent, and note how differently the same dollars register.

title: Volume and Average Volume
html:
Volume is the conviction behind the price

Price tells you where a stock went. Volume tells you how many people agreed to take it there. Volume is the total number of shares that changed hands over a period — one full day, or so far today. A move on heavy volume reflects broad participation; the same move on light volume can be a handful of orders pushing a thin tape around. Reading the two together is the first real skill of tape reading.

Definition.
Volume = total shares traded in the period. Average volume (commonly 30-day or 3-month) = the mean daily share count over that lookback window. Relative volume (RVOL) = Today's volume ÷ Average volume.

The problem of comparing apples to half-days

You cannot compare today's partial volume to a full-day average directly. If 12.4M shares have traded by noon and the 30-day average is 8.1M for a whole day, the headline already exceeds the average with half the session left. To judge it fairly you either project the full day or compare against the typical volume by this time of day.

Worked example

NOVA has traded 12.4M shares by noon (roughly the session midpoint). Its 30-day average full-day volume is 8.1M. Let us reason about it two ways.

NOVA · participation check
Volume so far (noon) 12.4M 30-day avg (full day) 8.1M Naive RVOL = 12.4 ÷ 8.1 = 1.53× Crude full-day projection = 12.4 × 2 = 24.8M Projected RVOL = 24.8 ÷ 8.1 = 3.06×

Even the conservative read — already at 1.53× a full day's average with hours to go — flags NOVA as a high-participation name today. The projection (a simple doubling at the midpoint) suggests it could finish near 3× normal. That is the kind of unusual activity that confirms a price move is being driven by real demand, not a quiet drift.

Insight.
Volume tends to be U-shaped through the day — heavy at the open, light midday, heavy into the close. A flat ×2 projection at lunch slightly UNDER-counts the typical day because the close is usually busy. Treat projections as rough, not gospel.

Reading the chart

The volume pane below sits under the price candles. Watch how the breakout bar is accompanied by a volume spike that towers over the preceding sessions.

The tall volume bar under the breakout candle is the visual signature of conviction. Compare its height to the cluster of shorter bars before it: that ratio is relative volume, read by eye. A breakout on a short volume bar is suspect; the same breakout on a bar two or three times the recent norm is the one worth respecting.

What different volume readings imply

  • High volume + price up — demand is winning; the advance has buyers behind it.
  • High volume + price down — supply is winning; distribution or capitulation.
  • Low volume + price up — a drift higher that may not hold; few committed buyers.
  • Volume spike with little net change — a battle, often near a turning point; lots of shares trading but neither side prevailing.
Pitfall.
Average volume itself drifts. After a stock joins an index, splits, or sees a news catalyst, its "normal" volume can reset to a new level. A 30-day average can lag a structural change for weeks. Glance at whether the average is trending up or down before you trust an RVOL number.
Do this.
Whenever a stock shows up on a mover list, check RVOL FIRST. A 5% move at 0.6× volume and a 5% move at 4× volume are not the same event, even though the percent change looks identical on the quote.
▸Try It

Goal: estimate relative volume and judge participation mid-session.

  1. Find a stock that is moving today. Note its current volume and its average (30-day or 3-month) volume.
  2. Estimate how far through the trading day you are, and project the full-day volume.
  3. Divide your projection by the average to get a rough RVOL. Is it above or below 1.5×?
  4. Pull up the chart with a volume pane. Compare today's bar to the prior two weeks by eye and confirm your number matches what you see.

title: Market Cap and Enterprise Value
html:
What is the company actually worth?

The share price tells you the cost of one share. To value the whole company you need two related numbers: market capitalization (the value of all the equity) and enterprise value (the value of the whole business including its debt, net of cash). Beginners stop at market cap; professionals quote EV, because EV is closer to what it would actually cost to buy the entire company.

Definition.
Market cap = Shares outstanding × Share price.
Enterprise value = Market cap + Total debt − Cash & equivalents.

Market cap: the equity slice

Market cap is the price of all the shares added together. It is the headline "size" number that sorts companies into large-cap, mid-cap, and small-cap. But it ignores the balance sheet entirely. Two companies can have identical market caps while one is debt-free and the other is buried in loans — clearly not equally valuable to an acquirer.

Worked example: ACME's market cap

ACME · market cap
Shares outstanding 500M Share price $142.50 Market cap = 500M × 142.50 = $71,250,000,000 = $71.25B

So the equity of ACME is worth about $71.25 billion. That places it as a large-cap. But to value the business, we must account for what it owes and what it holds in cash.

Enterprise value: the true takeover price

Imagine you buy every share for $71.25B. You now own the company — and its $12B of debt, which you are on the hook for. But you also gain its $5B of cash, which you could use to pay down some of that debt. So the real economic cost is higher than market cap by the debt and lower by the cash.

ACME · enterprise value
Market cap $71.25B + Total debt +$12.00B − Cash & equivalents −$5.00B Enterprise value = 71.25 + 12 − 5 = $78.25B

ACME's enterprise value is $78.25 billion — about $7B more than its market cap, because it carries net debt of $7B (12 owed minus 5 in cash). An acquirer effectively pays the equity price plus the net debt they inherit.

Insight.
When a company holds MORE cash than debt (net cash), enterprise value is LOWER than market cap. Cash-rich tech firms often have EV well below their market cap, which makes their EV-based multiples look cheaper than their P/E suggests.

Why EV matters for comparisons

Because EV neutralizes capital structure, it lets you compare companies financed very differently. A firm that funds itself with debt and one that funds itself with equity might have the same operating business; their P/E ratios would differ because of interest expense, but their EV/EBITDA (covered in Lesson 8) can be compared apples to apples. EV is the numerator in the valuation ratios that serious analysts trust most.

Same market cap, different EV
ACME cap 71.25, debt 12, cash 5 → EV 78.25 ZENITH cap 71.25, debt 2, cash 20 → EV 53.25 Gap = $25B despite identical market caps

Two companies, same equity value, $25B apart in true business value. The market cap line on a quote would call them twins; enterprise value tells you ZENITH's cash hoard makes it materially cheaper to actually own.

Pitfall.
Some quote pages show market cap using FLOAT instead of total shares outstanding, or omit preferred stock and minority interest from EV. For a precise EV you would add preferred equity and noncontrolling interest too. The simple Market cap + Debt − Cash version is enough for reading a quote, but know the full formula exists.
▸Try It

Goal: build market cap and enterprise value from a quote and balance sheet.

  1. Find a company's shares outstanding and current price. Multiply to confirm the market cap shown on the quote.
  2. From its latest balance sheet, note total debt and cash & equivalents.
  3. Compute EV = market cap + debt − cash. Is it above or below market cap? What does that tell you about net debt vs net cash?
  4. Repeat for a debt-heavy company and a cash-rich one. Compare how far each EV diverges from its market cap.

title: Shares Outstanding vs Float
html:
Not every share can actually trade

A quote may report two share counts that look similar but mean very different things: shares outstanding and float. The gap between them — shares that exist but are not available to the open market — is one of the most underappreciated drivers of how violently a stock moves.

Definition.
Shares outstanding = every share the company has issued (held by everyone — insiders, institutions, and the public). Float = shares outstanding − restricted/closely-held shares = the shares freely available to trade.

What gets subtracted to make the float

From total shares outstanding we remove shares that are not realistically for sale day to day: large insider and founder stakes, employee shares under lockup, government or strategic holdings, and other restricted blocks. What remains is the float — the supply that the buying and selling public is actually fighting over.

Worked example

ORCA · float worksheet
Shares outstanding 600M Insiders / locked-up −180M Float = 600M − 180M = 420M Float as % of total = 420 ÷ 600 = 70% Locked % = 180 ÷ 600 = 30%

ORCA has 600M shares outstanding but only 420M float, because insiders hold 180M locked up. Seventy percent of the company can trade; thirty percent is effectively off the market. That tighter tradable supply is what makes a float-aware trader pay attention.

Why a thin float moves faster

Price is set at the margin by supply and demand for the available shares. If only 420M shares are out there and a wave of buying arrives, there are fewer shares to satisfy it, so the price must rise further to coax holders into selling. The same dollar of demand pushes a small float much harder than a large one. Float is the denominator of how easily price gets moved.

$500M of buying hits two floats
BIG float 2.0B shares @ $50 → demand absorbed, small % move THIN float 40M shares @ $50 → same $500M is 25% of float, violent move Takeaway identical demand, opposite volatility

The same buy order is a drop in the ocean for a 2-billion-share float and a tidal wave for a 40-million-share float. This is the mechanical reason low-float stocks gap, spike, and reverse so sharply — and why they are favored by momentum traders and feared for their risk.

Insight.
Combine float with relative volume from Lesson 2. A small float TRADING multiple times its float in a day (high turnover) is the recipe for the most explosive intraday moves. Float tells you the supply; volume tells you how furiously it is being recycled.

Reading the chart

The chart shows a thin-float name making a sharp, near-parabolic run — the kind of move tight supply enables when demand floods in.

Notice how little consolidation there is on the way up: with few shares available, each new buyer must reach higher to find a seller, so the candles stack almost vertically. The flip side is brutal — when buying dries up, the same thin float offers no cushion of resting demand, and these moves reverse just as fast as they rose.

Pitfall.
Lockups expire. When an insider lockup ends, formerly restricted shares can flood into the float, expanding supply and pressuring price. A stock with a near-term lockup expiry can see its effective float jump overnight. Always check whether a thin float is about to get fatter.
Do this.
Before trading any fast mover, look up its float. Under ~50M shares is "low float" territory where moves are amplified and stops can be jumped. Size positions smaller and respect wider swings.
▸Try It

Goal: separate total shares from tradable float and gauge move sensitivity.

  1. Find a company's shares outstanding and its float (both are usually on the statistics tab of a quote).
  2. Subtract to find how many shares are restricted, and express the float as a percent of total.
  3. Find a large-cap with a multi-billion-share float and a small-cap under 50M float. Note the difference.
  4. Pull up the small-float stock's chart and look for the sharper, faster swings that tight supply produces.

title: 52-Week Range and Quote Moving Averages
html:
Where does today's price sit in its own history?

A single price means little without context. Two numbers on the quote supply that context cheaply: the 52-week range (the lowest and highest price over the past year) and the moving averages — usually the 50-day and 200-day — that the quote lists as round-number reference levels. Together they tell you whether a stock is near the top or bottom of its yearly arc and whether its medium and long trends are pointing the same way.

Definition.
52-week range = [lowest trade, highest trade] over the trailing year. Position in range = (Price − Low) ÷ (High − Low) × 100. A moving average is the mean closing price over the last N days, recalculated each day.

Placing price inside the 52-week range

The range gives you the boundaries; the position formula tells you where inside them you stand. A stock at the very top of its range (near 100%) is at fresh highs — strong, but with no overhead history of resistance. One near the bottom (near 0%) is beaten down — cheap-looking, but in a clear downtrend until proven otherwise.

Worked example

ACME · position in range
52-week low 98.10 52-week high 167.80 Price 142.50 Range width = 167.80 − 98.10 = 69.70 Above the low = 142.50 − 98.10 = 44.40 Position = 44.40 ÷ 69.70 = 0.637 ≈ 64%

ACME sits about 64% of the way up its 52-week range (the lesson's headline rounds this to roughly two-thirds). It is in the upper-middle of its year — recovered well off the 98.10 low, but still 25 points below the 167.80 high. There is room to run before fresh highs, and real support below from the prior consolidation.

The 50-day and 200-day on the quote

Quotes list the 50-day and 200-day moving averages as quick trend gauges. Price above a moving average means the average buyer over that window is in profit; price below means they are underwater. The 50-day reflects the medium-term trend; the 200-day reflects the long-term trend. Their relationship to price — and to each other — is the shorthand technicians read in a single glance.

ACME · price vs the MAs
Price 142.50 50-day MA 138.40 → price ABOVE by 4.10 (+3.0%) 200-day MA 145.90 → price BELOW by 3.40 (−2.3%) Read medium-term up, long-term not yet reclaimed

ACME is above its 50-day but below its 200-day — a classic recovery posture. The medium-term trend has turned up (price over the 50-day), but the stock has not yet reclaimed its long-term trend line (the 200-day). The 200-day at 145.90 becomes the next big test; clearing it would flip the long-term picture bullish.

Reading the chart

See how price rides above the faster 50-day line while still tucked beneath the slower 200-day. The 200-day acts as overhead resistance here — the level a recovering stock must punch through to confirm a full trend reversal. The yearly high and low brackets at the top and bottom frame just how much room exists in either direction. Until the 200-day is reclaimed, rallies into it are tests, not confirmations.

Insight.
The SLOPE of each moving average matters as much as price's position relative to it. A rising 50-day above a flattening 200-day is the early shape of a recovery; both falling is a downtrend; the 50-day crossing above the 200-day is the famous "golden cross" you will meet in the charting part.
Pitfall.
A stock making a NEW 52-week high has zero overhead supply but also no historical support nearby — pullbacks can be sharp because there are no old buyers to defend a level. "Near the highs" is strength, not safety.
▸Try It

Goal: locate a stock within its yearly range and trend structure.

  1. Find a stock's 52-week high, 52-week low, and current price. Compute its position-in-range percent.
  2. Note its 50-day and 200-day moving averages. Is price above or below each?
  3. Decide: medium-term trend up or down? Long-term up or down? Are they aligned?
  4. Chart it with the 50- and 200-day overlays and confirm your read by eye, then mark the nearest MA as the next test level.

title: EPS and the P/E Ratio (Trailing and Forward)
html:
Earnings per share and the price you pay for them

Two of the most quoted fundamental numbers are EPS (earnings per share) and the P/E ratio (price-to-earnings). EPS answers "how much profit does each share earn?" The P/E answers "how many years of those earnings am I paying for at today's price?" Master these and the rest of the valuation panel falls into place.

Definition.
EPS = Net income ÷ Shares outstanding. P/E = Share price ÷ EPS. Trailing P/E uses the LAST twelve months of actual EPS; forward P/E uses NEXT twelve months of ESTIMATED EPS.

From net income to EPS

EPS slices the company's total bottom-line profit across every share. It is the per-share earning power that the price is ultimately buying.

ACME · trailing EPS
Net income (last 12 mo) $3.6B Shares outstanding 500M Trailing EPS = 3,600M ÷ 500M = $7.20 per share

Each ACME share earned $7.20 over the trailing year. Now we ask what the market charges for that earning power.

Trailing P/E

ACME · trailing P/E
Price $142.50 Trailing EPS $7.20 Trailing P/E = 142.50 ÷ 7.20 = 19.8×

At a 19.8 trailing P/E, you pay about $19.80 for every $1 of last year's per-share earnings. Loosely, if earnings never grew and were all paid out, it would take ~19.8 years of earnings to recoup the price. Whether 19.8 is cheap or rich depends entirely on growth and on peers — a fast grower may deserve 30, a stagnant utility may warrant 12.

Forward P/E: paying for tomorrow

Markets price the future, not the past. The forward P/E swaps trailing earnings for analysts' estimate of next year's EPS. If ACME is expected to grow earnings to $8.65, the same price buys more future earnings, so the forward multiple is lower.

ACME · forward P/E
Price $142.50 Forward EPS estimate $8.65 Forward P/E = 142.50 ÷ 8.65 = 16.5× Implied EPS growth = (8.65 − 7.20) ÷ 7.20 = +20.1%

The forward P/E of 16.5 is lower than the trailing 19.8 precisely because earnings are expected to grow about 20%. A forward P/E below the trailing P/E signals expected growth; a forward P/E above trailing signals an expected earnings decline. The gap between the two ratios quietly encodes the market's growth assumption.

Insight.
Forward P/E is only as good as the estimates feeding it. Estimates drift and get revised. A seductively low forward P/E can simply mean analysts have not yet cut numbers that are about to fall — always check whether estimates are rising or being revised down.

Reading the chart

Earnings dates create some of the largest gaps on any chart. The example below shows a gap-up after a beat — the moment the EPS that feeds these ratios gets repriced.

The gap is the market rerating the stock the morning after results. If reported EPS comes in above the trailing figure, the denominator of the P/E rises and — even at the new higher price — the multiple can stay flat or fall. This is why a stock can rally hard on earnings yet become cheaper on a forward basis: price went up, but expected earnings went up more.

Pitfall.
A negative EPS makes P/E meaningless (often shown as "—" or "N/A"). Unprofitable companies have no P/E, which is why you fall back on price-to-sales and EV multiples (Lesson 8) for them. And beware GAAP vs adjusted EPS — quotes mix them, and "adjusted" can quietly exclude real costs.
▸Try It

Goal: compute EPS and both P/E flavors and read the implied growth.

  1. Find a company's trailing-twelve-month net income and shares outstanding. Divide to get trailing EPS.
  2. Divide the share price by that EPS for the trailing P/E. Compare it to the value on the quote.
  3. Find the forward EPS estimate and compute the forward P/E.
  4. Is forward below or above trailing? Translate that gap into the market's implied earnings growth or decline.

title: Dividend, Yield and Payout Ratio
html:
Getting paid to hold

Many companies return cash to shareholders as a dividend. The quote shows the annual dividend per share and the yield (that dividend as a percent of the price). One more number you compute yourself — the payout ratio — tells you whether the dividend is comfortably covered by earnings or stretched to the breaking point.

Definition.
Dividend yield = Annual dividend per share ÷ Share price × 100. Payout ratio = Annual dividend per share ÷ EPS × 100 (equivalently, total dividends ÷ net income).

Yield: the cash return at today's price

Yield expresses the dividend as an annual percentage of what you pay for the stock. It is the income return before any price change. Note that yield moves inversely to price: if the dividend is fixed and the price falls, the yield rises — which is why a soaring yield can be a warning, not a gift.

ACME · dividend yield
Annual dividend / share $2.40 Share price $142.50 Yield = 2.40 ÷ 142.50 = 0.01684 × 100 = 1.68%

ACME pays a 1.68% yield — modest, typical of a growth-leaning company that prefers to reinvest. A retiree-favorite utility might yield 4-5%; a young tech firm might pay nothing at all. Yield alone never tells you if the payout is safe; for that we need the payout ratio.

Payout ratio: is the dividend covered?

The payout ratio asks what fraction of earnings is being handed out as dividends. A low ratio means the dividend is well covered with room to grow; a ratio near or above 100% means the company is paying out everything (or more than) it earns — unsustainable without dipping into reserves or debt.

ACME · payout ratio
Annual dividend / share $2.40 EPS $7.20 Payout ratio = 2.40 ÷ 7.20 = 0.333 × 100 = 33% Retained for growth = 67%

ACME pays out just 33% of earnings, keeping 67% to reinvest, pay down debt, or buy back stock. That is a comfortable, well-covered dividend with clear room to grow. As a rough guide: under ~50% is conservative, 50-75% is moderate, and above ~80% leaves little cushion if earnings stumble.

Insight.
A LOW payout ratio plus rising earnings is the profile of a future dividend grower. The dividend can climb even if the company simply holds its payout ratio steady while EPS expands. Income investors prize this combination over a high but stagnant yield.

The yield trap

How a falling price inflates yield
Start $2.40 div, $142.50 price → 1.68% Price drops to 80 $2.40 ÷ 80 → 3.00% Price drops to 48 $2.40 ÷ 48 → 5.00% Warning rising yield from a SINKING price, not a raise

If ACME's price halved on bad news while the dividend stayed at $2.40, the yield would balloon toward 5% — not because management got generous, but because the price collapsed. A suspiciously high yield often flags a market betting the dividend will be CUT. Cross-check every juicy yield against the payout ratio and the earnings trend before trusting it.

Pitfall.
Some quotes show a TRAILING dividend (what was actually paid over the past year) and others a FORWARD/indicated dividend (the current rate annualized). After a recent raise or cut these differ. Also, special one-time dividends can inflate a trailing figure that will not repeat.
Do this.
For any income stock, compute the payout ratio yourself and check whether the dividend has been raised, held, or cut over recent years. A long record of raises with a sub-60% payout is the hallmark of a durable dividend.
▸Try It

Goal: compute yield and payout ratio and judge dividend safety.

  1. Find a dividend-paying stock's annual dividend per share and current price. Compute the yield and match it to the quote.
  2. Find its EPS and compute the payout ratio. Is it conservative, moderate, or stretched?
  3. Imagine the price falling 30%. Recompute the yield to see how a sinking price inflates it.
  4. Check the company's dividend history — raised, held, or cut? Combine that with the payout ratio to rate the dividend's durability.

title: Beta, Volatility and the Rest of the Ratios
html:
The rest of the statistics panel, decoded

Below the headline numbers sits a panel of ratios that look intimidating but each answer one simple question. This lesson defines and computes the lot: beta and volatility, plus P/B, P/S, PEG, EV/EBITDA, ROE, profit margin, short interest and days-to-cover. We will run one fictional company, ACME, through every one.

Beta and volatility

Definition.
Beta measures a stock's sensitivity to the overall market. Beta = 1 moves with the market; >1 amplifies it; <1 dampens it; negative moves opposite. Volatility is the size of a stock's own price swings (often annualized standard deviation of returns).
ACME · beta read
Beta 1.25 Market moves +1.0% → expect ACME ≈ +1.25% Market moves −1.0% → expect ACME ≈ −1.25% Read 25% MORE swing than the index, both ways

A 1.25 beta means ACME tends to move 25% more than the market in either direction — more reward in rallies, more pain in selloffs. Beta is about relative volatility versus the index; it says nothing about company-specific risk, which volatility and the fundamentals below capture.

P/B and P/S — price against book and sales

ACME · P/B and P/S
Price 142.50   Book value/share 33.93 P/B = 142.50 ÷ 33.93 = 4.2× Market cap 71.25B   Revenue 13.97B P/S = 71.25 ÷ 13.97 = 5.1×

P/B 4.2 means the market values ACME at 4.2× the accounting net worth of its assets — common for asset-light firms with valuable intangibles. P/S 5.1 means you pay $5.10 per $1 of annual sales; P/S is the go-to multiple when earnings are thin or negative, since revenue is always positive.

PEG — valuation adjusted for growth

ACME · PEG
Trailing P/E 19.8 Expected EPS growth 18%/yr PEG = 19.8 ÷ 18 = 1.1

The PEG of 1.1 divides the P/E by the growth rate. A PEG near 1 is the classic "fairly priced for its growth" benchmark; well below 1 hints at a bargain relative to growth; well above 2 suggests you are paying up. PEG rescues high P/E growth stocks that look expensive on P/E alone.

EV/EBITDA — the capital-structure-neutral multiple

ACME · EV/EBITDA
Enterprise value (Lesson 3) 78.25B EBITDA 5.59B EV/EBITDA = 78.25 ÷ 5.59 = 14×

Using the enterprise value from Lesson 3 over EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, amortization), EV/EBITDA of 14 compares the whole business to its core cash earnings, ignoring how it is financed. It is the multiple acquirers and analysts lean on most because it neutralizes debt and tax differences.

ROE and profit margin — quality of the business

ACME · returns and margin
Net income 3.6B   Equity 16.36B ROE = 3.6 ÷ 16.36 = 22% Net income 3.6B   Revenue 13.97B Net margin = 3.6 ÷ 13.97 = 24%

ROE 22% says ACME earns 22 cents of profit per dollar of shareholder equity — a strong return on the capital owners have invested. Net margin 24% says 24 cents of every sales dollar reaches the bottom line — a highly profitable operation. High, stable ROE and margins are hallmarks of a quality, competitively-moated business.

Short interest and days-to-cover

Definition.
Short interest % = shares sold short ÷ float. Days-to-cover (short ratio) = shares short ÷ average daily volume — how many normal trading days shorts would need to buy back their position.
ACME · short metrics
Float (Lesson 4 style) 420M   Shares short 25.2M Short interest = 25.2 ÷ 420 = 6% Avg daily volume 7.2M Days-to-cover = 25.2 ÷ 7.2 = 3.5 days

6% short interest at 3.5 days-to-cover is moderate. High short interest with high days-to-cover is fuel for a short squeeze: if price rises, shorts scramble to buy back, but it would take 3.5 days of normal volume to unwind — that buying pressure can accelerate a rally.

Reading the chart

When a heavily-shorted stock breaks higher, the forced buying from shorts covering adds demand on top of normal buyers, which is why squeezes can look so violent. The RSI pane racing into overbought reflects that crowded, one-directional rush. Days-to-cover tells you how much pent-up covering remains as ammunition.

Pitfall.
No single ratio decides anything. A low PEG with deteriorating margins, or a high ROE driven purely by heavy debt (leverage flatters ROE), can mislead. Read the panel as a SYSTEM — cross-check valuation (P/E, P/S, EV/EBITDA, PEG) against quality (ROE, margins) and positioning (short interest, beta).
▸Try It

Goal: compute and interpret the full ratio panel for one company.

  1. Pick a company and gather: price, book value/share, market cap, revenue, EBITDA, net income, equity, P/E, growth rate, float, shares short, and average volume.
  2. Compute P/B, P/S, PEG, EV/EBITDA, ROE, and net margin from the formulas above. Match each to the quote.
  3. Compute short interest as a percent of float and days-to-cover.
  4. Write one sentence summarizing whether the stock looks cheap or rich, high or low quality, and crowded or not — using the panel as a whole.
